text: A trocar is placed at the umbilicus for diagnostic laparoscopy, followed by the placement of three robotic operating arms at the same level.
An airflow device is positioned along the anterior axillary line, cephalad at the umbilicus near the costal margin, as part of the surgical setup.
The surgeon uses a cut-down technique to place an applied port, completing the setup for the robotic-assisted pancreaticoduodenectomy.
The surgeon positions a port caudal to the level of the umbilicus, between the umbilicus and the midclavicular line on the right side.
An assistant works through one of the trocars, which is ultimately used for tissue extraction during the pancreaticoduodenectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins the operation by taking down the falciform ligament, which is not routinely done unless it obstructs the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a reinforcement material to cover the biliary and pancreatic anastomosis, ensuring secure closure and protection of these surgical connections.
The surgeon opens the gastro-hepatic omentum in a stellate fashion and carries the dissection down to the common hepatic artery, where an enlarged lymph node is identified and removed.
The surgeon clips the right gastric artery twice and divides it, followed by clipping and dividing the gastroduodenal artery. Subsequently, a Coker maneuver is performed to mobilize the duodenum.
The surgeon initiates the Coker maneuver near the bile duct, revealing the inferior vena cava in the surgical field.
The surgeon frees the duodenum from the inferior vena cava, proceeding in a caudal direction, until the left renal vein is clearly visualized.
The surgeon dissects and takes down the ligament of Treitz, advancing the jejunum under the root of the mesentery before dividing the jejunum.
The surgeon opens the gastrocolic omentum and lifts the stomach ventrally. Dissection proceeds through the gastroepiploic artery and vein, followed by division of the duodenum.

The surgeon dissects along the inferior border of the pancreas to identify the pyramesenteric vein. The pancreas is lifted off the pyramesenteric vein and portal vein, with division performed using a hook cautery, vessel sealer, or scissors with energy.

The surgeon opens the mesentery for the transsection of the jejunum, dividing the mesentery as the dissection progresses toward the area where the superior mesenteric vein passes beneath the pancreas.
The surgeon performs dissection along the superior mesenteric artery, skeletonizing it, while mobilizing the portal vein away from the artery. This creates a clean surgical field for lymphadenectomy.
The surgeon continues the dissection to free the gallbladder, carefully dissecting across the common hepatic duct to remove the gallbladder en bloc with the specimen.
The surgeon clips the cystic artery twice proximally and once distally, followed by clipping the cystic duct, securing these structures for subsequent removal.
The surgeon continues dissection along the common hepatic duct, identifying and clipping a notably large branch from the portal vein.
A stent is visible within the bile duct, indicating its placement for maintaining ductal patency during the procedure.
The surgeon places the specimen into an extraction bag and removes it through the port site, completing the specimen retrieval process.
After the specimen is removed and sent to pathology, the surgeon advances the bowel under the root of the mesentery and constructs the bilioenteric anastomosis using a running 3-0 B-lock suture.

The surgeon ties a knot at the nine o'clock position on the bile duct, running a suture from inside out of the duct to outside in on the bowel. The suture is carried over to the three o'clock position on the duct, and another suture is started at nine o'clock, going outside in on the duct and inside out on the bowel. The sutures are positioned across from each other and tied, forming a bilioenteric anastomosis.

The surgeon constructs a two-layer pancreatic jejunosomy, bringing the full thickness of the pancreas into two seromuscular purchases of the bowel, creating a small valley for the gland. A duct-to-mucosa anastomosis is formed using interrupted sutures, with four or five sutures placed outside-in on the bowel and inside-out on the duct, tied as they are placed. The second layer is completed along the ventral aspect of the pancreas using a running V-lock suture.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis between the pancreatic duct and the enterotomy, running the ventral layer of the two-layer anastomosis using three OV-lock sutures. The corner sutures, connecting the posterior and ventral layers, are tied securely.
The surgeon uses sutures to invert the staple line on the small bowel, followed by reconstructing the ligament of Treitz with several interrupted V-lock sutures.

The surgeon identifies the small bowel and the afferent and efferent limbs for the duodenal jejunostomy. The small bowel and duodenum are opened, and the staple line on the duodenum is excised during the anastomosis. The posterior layer is constructed with sutures placed inside out on the duodenum and outside in on the small bowel, running until reaching three o'clock on the duodenum. The staple line is removed, ensuring the antrum appears pink. The ventral layer reconstruction is a one-layer anastomosis, starting at nine o'clock and sewing to three o'clock on the duodenum, with sutures tied across from each other. A drain is placed under the bile duct, and the anastomosis is wrapped with the falciform ligament.
